planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost at 70–75°F in darkness — vinca seeds germinate best with no light; transplant only when nights are consistently above 50°F in full sun in well-drained soil. In zone 9–12 plant container transplants outdoors year-round.

directions: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost at 70–75°F in complete darkness — vinca seeds require dark conditions for germination — and transplant after nights consistently stay above 50°F in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; annual vinca (Catharanthus roseus, not to be confused with Vinca minor) is the definitive summer annual for hot, humid, full-sun conditions, producing continuous, five-petaled flowers in white, pink, red, coral, lavender, and bicolors from late spring through frost without deadheading. Outstanding heat and drought tolerance makes it the top-performing annual for the Gulf Coast, Deep South, and other high-heat-and-humidity regions where other annuals fail mid-season. Do not overwater or plant in heavy, wet soil — this is the primary cause of aerial phytophthora root rot, the most serious disease problem. 'Cora' and 'Titan' series have excellent heat performance and disease resistance for southern gardens.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Direct-sow large seeds 1 inch deep after last frost date in full sun in any well-drained soil; four o'clocks germinate quickly and grow rapidly to blooming size. In zone 8–11, the tuberous roots can be left in the ground year-round as perennials.

directions: Direct-sow large seeds 1 inch deep after last frost in full sun to partial shade in any well-drained soil; four o'clocks live up to their name by opening their trumpet-shaped flowers in late afternoon and keeping them open through the night and into the following morning — the evening fragrance is sweet and strong, making them outstanding plants for evening gardens, patios, and porches. Flowers come in white, yellow, pink, red, magenta, and spectacular multicolored combinations where the markings seem to defy simple genetics, often producing multiple colors on one plant or even striped and blotched flowers. The tuberous roots are perennial in zone 7–11 and can be dug in fall like dahlias and stored overwinter in colder climates. The large black seeds self-sow prolifically and can become weedy in mild climates; be aware that all plant parts are toxic if ingested.

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds on prepared soil surface in full sun after last frost — seeds need light to germinate — or refrigerate seeds for 2 weeks before sowing to improve germination rate. Spider flower self-seeds freely and will naturalize in a sunny bed.

directions: Direct-sow seeds on prepared soil surface in full sun after last frost — seeds need light and should not be covered — and thin to 18–24 inches; spider flower grows quickly into a bold, tall annual with distinctive globe-shaped heads of pink, white, or purple flowers with extraordinarily long, spider-like stamens that protrude dramatically beyond the petals, giving each bloom its spider-like appearance. Blooms continuously from midsummer through frost and self-seeds so prolifically that it naturalizes readily in a sunny border, reappearing every year without replanting. Highly drought-tolerant and unfazed by summer heat; the flowers are excellent for cutting and attract swallowtail butterflies, sphinx moths, and hummingbirds. The sticky stems and leaves have a strong scent that most people find disagreeable up close; plant away from areas where the foliage will be touched or brushed against.

planting.note: Press seeds onto moist seed-starting mix surface without covering — nicotiana seeds need light to germinate — at 65–75°F; start 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant after all danger of frost has passed in full sun to partial shade.

directions: Press seeds onto moist seed-starting mix surface in bright light without covering at 65–75°F — seeds need light for germination; start 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant after all frost danger passes in full sun to partial shade. Flowering tobacco produces an extended succession of tubular, star-shaped flowers in white, pink, lime-green, red, and crimson from early summer until frost, with the white-flowered forms releasing one of the most intensely sweet fragrances in the evening garden, attracting sphinx moths and hummingbirds after dark. Modern dwarf hybrids (Sensation series) are day-neutral and odorless; for fragrance seek out the species or older heirloom selections. It tolerates partial shade better than most summer annuals and self-seeds freely where happy.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Scatter seeds on the soil surface in full sun after last frost in very well-drained, sandy to lean soil; moss rose performs best in the poorest, driest, hottest conditions available — rich, moist soil produces leggy growth with few flowers.

directions: Scatter seeds on the surface of very well-drained, lean, sandy soil in the hottest, sunniest spot in the garden after last frost; moss rose is the definitive annual for dry, hot, sandy, poor soils where nothing else will grow, producing brilliant, multi-petaled, rose-like flowers in every shade of red, orange, yellow, pink, white, and bicolor from early summer through hard frost. It thrives on neglect — poor, dry, gravelly soil in full sun that bakes in summer heat produces the finest bloom; rich, moist, fertilized soil causes rank vegetative growth with sparse flowers. The flowers close in cloudy conditions and at night; choose heat-tolerant varieties with double flowers that stay open longer. Self-seeds modestly; excellent for gaps in paving, gravel gardens, slopes, and difficult dry banks.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost at 70–80°F, soaking them in water for 3–4 days before sowing to improve germination; transplant after all frost danger has passed in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil.

directions: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost at 70–80°F — soaking seeds in warm water for 3–4 days before planting improves germination rate — and transplant after all frost danger passes in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; globe amaranth produces perfectly round, papery, clover-like flower heads in vivid magenta-purple, pink, white, red, and orange from early summer through frost with exceptional heat, drought, and humidity tolerance that makes it outstanding for Gulf Coast and southern summer gardens. The papery flower heads dry on the plant and retain their color for months, making it one of the best everlasting annuals for dried arrangements — cut stems before flowers are fully open for the best dried color. Highly deer-resistant; very low maintenance once established.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost at 60–65°F in individual pots as statice has a long taproot that resents transplanting; transplant carefully after hardening off in full sun in well-drained, sandy to moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost in individual pots — statice has a long taproot that resents root disturbance — at 60–65°F, then transplant carefully after last frost in full sun in well-drained, sandy to moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil. Statice blooms for months with branched stems covered in tiny papery flowers surrounded by persistent colored calyces in purple-lavender, white, rose, yellow, and apricot, and is the definitive everlasting cut flower — the calyces retain their vivid color for years when dried at room temperature upside-down in a warm, dark, ventilated space. Outstanding for coastal gardens as it is highly salt-spray tolerant. In zone 9–10 it can be sown in fall for a spring bloom season.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost at 65–75°F and transplant after last frost in full sun in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile soil; in zone 8–10 it often overwinters and behaves as a short-lived perennial.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost at 65–75°F — do not cover seeds as light improves germination — and transplant after last frost in full sun in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile soil; dusty miller is grown entirely for its deeply cut, velvety, intensely silver-white foliage that serves as a luminous contrast plant with any flower color in the summer border. It thrives in hot, dry conditions, tolerates coastal salt spray, and looks exceptionally fresh paired with purple, red, or deep blue flowers. Remove yellow flower heads as they develop if grown for foliage effect. In zone 8–10 plants often overwinter and become woody short-lived perennials; take cuttings in late summer to maintain vigorous young plants. The genus was recently reclassified from Senecio cineraria to Jacobaea maritima.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds in fall in partial shade to full sun in moist, well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic soil; cold-moist stratification over winter significantly improves spring germination. Container transplants may be planted in spring or fall.

directions: Direct-sow seeds in fall or plant container transplants in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; blue star is a stunning native perennial of eastern streambanks and woodland edges, producing clouds of small, pale-blue star-shaped flowers in late spring above willowy, fresh-green foliage. The foliage is attractive all season and turns brilliant golden-yellow in fall, giving the plant a remarkable three-season display. Established plants are long-lived, drought-tolerant, deer-resistant, and essentially pest-free — a nearly perfect low-maintenance perennial. It grows slowly to a substantial clump over several years; dividing is not necessary and the plant should be left undisturbed once established. An exceptional combination plant for partial shade alongside hostas, ferns, and native woodland wildflowers.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions 1–2 inches deep in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; false Solomon's seal slowly spreads by rhizomes to form a naturalistic colony under deciduous trees.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions 1–2 inches deep in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade under deciduous trees; false Solomon's seal is a beautiful and underused native woodland plant with arching stems of large, oval, ribbed leaves similar to Solomon's seal, but differing in its terminal plumes of creamy-white, lightly fragrant flowers that appear in spring rather than the axillary bells of the related Polygonatum. The flower clusters are followed by green berries that ripen to striking speckled red-and-white in fall and are relished by thrushes, grouse, and other woodland birds. It spreads slowly by rhizomes to form a graceful, naturalistic colony in humus-rich woodland soil — one of the most rewarding native groundcover plants for the moist deciduous woodland garden. Reclassified from Smilacina racemosa to Maianthemum racemosum in recent botanical treatments.
